title: Predictable weekly schedule
explain: Knowing your school AND your sport gives away your location at fixed times every week. Practice usually runs the same days, same time, same gate.

title: Your real age, confirmed
explain: Your username contains what looks like a birth year. Predators tailor their approach to age — e.g. pretending to be 1–2 years older to feel 'safe'.

title: Vulnerability window — you walk alone
explain: A post mentioning walking home reveals an unsupervised moment on a predictable route. Combined with school + practice times, an attacker can be waiting at a specific corner.

title: Location narrowed to ~1–2 km
explain: A neighbourhood mention plus your school cuts your possible home location down to a small radius. Cross-reference with a sports-club catchment and it's smaller still.

title: Likely password-recovery answers
explain: Pet names and sibling names are the two most common security-question answers. With both, an attacker can attempt account recovery on your email or socials.

title: A safe-feeling place to 'bump into' you
explain: A favourite hangout gives a predator a low-risk, public location to engineer a 'coincidental' first meeting — making them feel like a familiar face later.

title: Real name reachable in <5 minutes
explain: School yearbooks, team rosters, and tagged photos let an attacker match your handle to a real name. Most schools' team pages list full names publicly.

title: Emotional opening for grooming
explain: A post showing loneliness or conflict signals an emotional gap a predator can step into ('I get it, my family was the same…'). It's the most common opening line.

title: Even this much is a starting point
explain: Anything you share publicly — even just a handle and a sport — gives an attacker a personalised opener. The conversation feels less random, so trust builds faster.
